observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require:

The CTO to:
Immediately forward to the OCAT the supporting documents for the recording of necessary adjusting entries;
Regularly coordinate with the OCAT to facilitate reconciliation of cashbook balances with the OCAT SLs for CIB accounts; and
Promptly communicate noted discrepancies with the OCAT so adjustments, if any, can be recorded in a timely manner both in the books and BRS.

The OCAT to:
Ensure that BRS are prepared for all active bank accounts and reconciling items identified in the reports are promptly recorded in the books; and
Ensure that identified reconciling items in the BRS are immediately adjusted in the books to prevent accumulation of unadjusted variances between records of the OCAT, CTO and AGDB balances.

We recommended that Management:
Direct all concerned AOs to submit the liquidation reports with supporting documents (SDs) for all outstanding CAs by issuing demand letters particularly for due and demandable advances as of 
December 31, 2024;
Ensure that O&E who will retire, resign or transfer to other agencies settle their accountabilities before issuing them clearance certificates.  Ascertain the actual employment status of those with no information as to whether they are still active or already separated from the service; and
Refrain from granting additional CAs to O&E without first liquidating their previous CAs pursuant to Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that Management require:
The OCAT to:
Maintain SLs for all receivable accounts to ensure that detailed records support the bulk amounts reflected in the financial statements.
Immediately coordinate and reconcile with the concerned NGAs and GOCCs to confirm the unconfirmed balances and establish the correct amount for the affected accounts.
Prepare adjusting entries to correct misstatements identified upon thorough evaluation of the transactions;
Conduct a regular review and reconciliation of receivables, including monthly or quarterly reporting, to ensure the accuracy of balances in the financial statements; and
Coordinate and reconcile with the Patrimonial Unit of the City General Services Office's (CGSO) to establish the correct balances for the leased properties of the City.
The MUSO to:
Submit a complete and up-to-date schedule of receivables, including collections and ageing reports pertaining to the Land for the Landless program, to OCAT on a timely basis.

We recommended that Management direct:
The OCAT to:
Coordinate with the CGSO to address inconsistencies in the PPE items and properly account for them in the accounting records;
Prepare the necessary information required by COA Circular 
No. 2020-006, and conduct the procedures provided therein for the implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE in the books; and
Prepare adjusting entries to correct the misclassified Medical Equipment items.
The CGSO to:
Coordinate with the OCAT for the reconciliation of PPE records against the RPCPPE, and address issues pertaining to PPEs found at stations during the physical count; and
Prepare the requirements per COA Circular No. 2020-006 and expedite the procedures outlined therein for the one-time cleansing of PPE.
Henceforth, strictly comply with the proper recording of PPE accounts as guided by Paragraph 14 of IPSAS 1, and Section 6.3 of COA Circular 
No. 2020-006.

We recommended that Management direct the OCAT to:
Analyze the CIP account and make appropriate adjustments to attain its fair presentation;
Regularly coordinate with CGSO and other implementing offices to timely recognize the completion of projects and consider the formulation of guidelines that will address issues such as delayed processing of final billings by contractors to facilitate the timely and proper accounting treatment of CIP transactions; and
To prepare and maintain the required CIPLC.

We recommended and management agreed that the OCAT will reconcile the confirmed payables balances from suppliers/contractors with the recorded amounts per books, and prepare the necessary adjustments to correct the figures.

We recommended that the Management direct the OCAT to:
Conduct a comprehensive reconciliation of the account by matching withheld taxes with actual remittances and analyze long-outstanding unremitted taxes from prior years and SLs with abnormal balances.  Subsequently, draw adjusting entries as necessary with sufficient supporting documents;
Maintain a record that monitors the status of any unpaid employee compensation where taxes are already withheld; and
Ensure that controls are in place to warrant that taxes are correctly computed and withheld, and that these are immediately remitted to the BIR when due.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the DPOs to:
Ensure the timely submission of updated information of City employees to GSIS, such as new salary rates, employment status, positions, and other pertinent information to minimize the occurrence of mismatched records;
Promptly address deficiencies noted which cause the mismatch of data, and coordinate/report to the CPO all actions taken and results of such actions regarding the request for updates of employees’ information before the GSIS;
Regularly reconcile, together with AAOs, their records with the OCAT and CPO to promptly establish the actual amount withheld from employees for immediate recording and remittance and loan amortizations to the GSIS; and
Require AAOs to submit to OCAT and CPO complete and accurate records of employees with existing loan accounts and mismatched records with the GSIS for monitoring and reconciliation purposes.
Require the CPO to:
Require the AAOs to ensure that monthly amortizations shall be deducted from the monthly salary of employees;
Maintain a detailed and comprehensive database of all employees with existing loan accounts and mismatched records with the GSIS for monitoring and reconciliation purposes; and
Regularly coordinate with the DPOs, AAOs, and OCAT to address the root cause and mitigate the discrepancies/deficiencies noted.
Require the OCAT to:
Address and correct the accounting errors noted in Paragraph 3(a); and
Expedite, together with DPOs, AAOs, and CPO, the reconciliation of members’ records with the GSIS to avoid the discrepancies/deficiencies noted.
Strengthen coordination among departments, streamline the remittance process, and implement more effective monitoring and reconciliation procedures;
Provide uniform policy/guidelines to all personnel involved from OCAT, CPO, DPOs, and AAOs on the proper procedures for handling GSIS contributions, loan repayments, and member updates to ensure that all employees are aware of their responsibilities and that there is a consistent approach to managing GSIS-related tasks across departments; and
Henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 14, 15 and 16 of RA No. 8291 and its RIRR and GSIS Memorandum Circular No. 027, s. 2021, dated May 19, 2021.

We recommended that Management:
Remit the P34,589,935.79 premium contributions and loan amortizations withheld from employees upon due validation of the amount;
Reconcile and draw necessary adjusting entries for the P123,096.14 abnormal balance; and
Strictly comply with Section 3, Rule VII of the IRR of RA No. 9679 dated           July 21, 2009, on the remittances of premium contributions and loan payments.

We recommended that –
The OCAT reconcile their records to determine the nature and necessary details of the unaccounted balances in the Due to PhilHealth account, or prepare necessary JEV to adjust the books, accordingly;
The Management ensure regular remittance of premium contributions in accordance with Sections 18(b), Rule III, Title III of the Revised IRR of the RA No. 7875, as amended by RA No. 9241 and RA No. 10606 and PhilHealth Circular No. 0001, s. 2014; and
The Management to strengthen its internal control systems in the management of its Due to PhilHealth account through the: a) conduct of periodic verification of the list of its employees against their records on PhilHealth; b) requiring internal deadline on the submission of payrolls of the offices to the OCAT; and c) formulating proper and systematic tracking of documents from preparation, transmittal, payment and recording of remittances.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the CTO to coordinate with and update the OCAT on the status of these checks for proper recording in the books at year-end;
Require the OCAT to submit the 2407 DVs and related supporting documents to this Office for review and post-audit, upon payment; and
Henceforth, ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, relative to the submission of reports.

We recommended that Management require the CCPC to:
Revisit the existing planning and monitoring procedures currently in place for LCPC PPAs to determine and improve on weak areas that hinder the efficient implementation of programs/projects to enable the CCPC attain the Council’s objective of protecting the rights and welfare of disadvantaged children; and
Adopt the provisions of the DILG MC Nos. 2021-039 and 2008-126.

We recommended that Management:
Through the DPS and SWM Board, for SWM ordinances and SWM Plans, to:
Fast-track the revision of outdated SWM ordinances and strengthen penalties for non-compliance to align with evolving national policies and current environmental challenges of the City; and
Formulate a mechanism for the regular review and updating of the SWM Plan to ensure compliance with legal requirements and responsiveness to evolving waste management challenges.
Implement aggressive IEC campaigns to increase public awareness and compliance, and adjust engagement targets to reflect demographic changes.
Direct the City Council to take the necessary steps to operationalize the Manila CENRO to fulfill its environmental mandate.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Continue maximizing the utilization/implementation of the GAD Plan and Budget to attain its objective in accordance with Item 2.3 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2013-01 dated July 18, 2013;
Ensure that the HGDG tool is fully integrated into the budgeting and planning processes for all major programs, particularly those aimed at enhancing gender responsiveness.  Organize training sessions and workshops for key decision-makers, including management and finance staff, to familiarize them with the HGDG tool and its proper application in gender-responsive budgeting
Ensure that gender issues in the GAD AR are categorized into 'client-focused' and 'organizational-focused' to improve clarity, tracking, and effectiveness of gender-responsive programs.
Ensure timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and the AR for timely review of the same.
Strictly observe the guidelines on GAD PPAs under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Accountant immediately enforce the settlement of all audit suspensions, disallowances, and charges in compliance with Sections 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of COA Circular 
No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, and Management be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting and auditing rules and regulations in the processing of claims to avoid possible audit suspensions and disallowances.

Specify in the schedule of payables the correct addresses of all the concerned creditors.

Establish the accountability for and recover the excess payment of emergency subsidy and FA; and
In coordination with the DSWD, supply the missing beneficiary information necessary in the full validation of the payments of the subject emergency subsidy/FA.

Prospectively, improve the implementation of the DILG-approved GAD PPAs; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management require:
The City Accountant and the City Treasurer to conduct review and analyses of their respective records to determine the nature of each account and come up with reconciled and correct balances of the CiB accounts for all funds, reconcile with the records of the depository banks, and prepare adjustments, if warranted; and
The OCAT to submit to the Audit Team complete SL to facilitate the review of the CiB account.
The non-preparation/submission of BRSs for the GF and the delayed submission of BRSs for the SEF and TF by 51 to 155 days and 44 to 288 days, respectively, were contrary to Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996, hence, correctness of the CiB account could not be verified and validated.
Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 requires the Local Accountant within 10 days from receipt of Bank Statements (BS) to reconcile the same with the GL and to prepare the BRS in five copies.  The triplicate copy to be furnished to the City Treasurer.
Section 3.4 of the same Circular states that the duplicate and quadruplicate copies of the BRS including the paid checks, original copies of debit/credit memos, shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned for verification within 10 days from receipt of BS.
The OCAT had been preparing BRSs for the 12 depository accounts for the three funds maintained with the LBP and DBP as follows:
As of December 31, 2018, the latest BRSs submitted under the GF-LBP and DBP was only for November 2016 and December 2017, respectively, which were received by the City Auditor’s Office (CAO) on September 19, 2018 and February 13, 2019, or a delay of 644 and 395 days, respectively, thus, did not facilitate the timely review of the BRSs.
The delayed reconciliation of the bank and book records and preparation of the corresponding BRS resulted in the late detection of errors and omissions and further accumulation of bank and book reconciling items for the GF.
Likewise, the 10 BRSs for the SEF and TF covering the period January to December 2018 for the different bank accounts under the CIB-LCCA accounts were not submitted to the CAO within the prescribed period or delayed by 51 to 155 days and 44 to 288 days, respectively.
Per interview with the OCAT, as in the previous years’, the late submission of the BRSs was due to the late receipt of the BSs from the depository banks for up to three months by their Office.
The timely review of the BRSs for the GF, SEF and TF was not undertaken due to the non/late submission of the same, hence, any deficiency noted was not immediately brought to the attention of the Management.
The City Accountant submitted the BRSs on May 20 and 22, 2019 for the DBP and LBP depository accounts under the GF covering the period January to December 2018 and January to May 2018, respectively. She stated that the delay in the preparation and submission of BRSs for SEF and TF was due to the late transmittal of the BSs from the depository banks by three to four months, thus, she requested for soft copies of the same in excel format to facilitate the preparation of the BRSs but instead, they were provided in portable document format, hence, the need for manual conversion.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Management require the OCAT to prioritize and fast track the preparation of the BRSs for the LBP and DBP depository accounts under the GF and immediately record all reconciling items noted, and submit on time the BRSs for the GF, SEF and TF to the CAO pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Management require:
The City Accountant to-
Effect the adjustment of the book reconciling items for the current year in the amount of P4.692 million;
Undertake a thorough review of the BRSs such that reconciling items are clearly presented therein to facilitate verification by the Audit Team; and
The City Accountant and City Treasurer to secure the pertinent documentation from the concerned depository banks to support the adjustment of the reconciling items in the books pertaining to prior years in the amount of P13.470 million.

We recommended that the OCAT and City Treasurer review their respective records vis-à-vis the CRRs and  prepare  the necessary adjustments for reconciling items and errors in mathematical computations to come up with an accurate and reconciled balances.

We recommended that the Management require the LFC to:
Study thoroughly the reasonableness of the projected income to be collected to arrive at a more realistic appropriation; and
Conduct a periodic review of its collection efficiency to keep track of the revenue/income accounts that do not meet the revenue targets for appropriate remedial action.

We recommended that Management direct:
a.	The City Budget Officer to stop the practice of requiring the City Treasurer to issue certification on the availability of cash on WFP and Request for Allotment for the fourth quarter of the budget year which is not a customary procedure of the LGU;
b.	The City Treasurer to refrain from:
Certifying cash availability in the Purchase Request to support the incurrence of obligations/payables at year-end in the absence of actual and sufficient cash; and
Depositing in the GF depository account the fund/s received/transferred which are intended for the TF.
Management comments/actions:
The City Budget Officer stated that for GF, in order to avoid cash deficit, it is the policy of their Office to require certification as to the availability of cash to be issued by the CTO on the WFP and Request for Allotment during the fourth quarter of the year. Request for allotments on Continuing Appropriations for CO is also required to be cleared with the CTO and OCAT as to availability of cash and fund, respectively.
The City Treasurer informed that the main duties of their Office are collection of taxes, fees and other charges and issues check for processed and duly approved disbursement voucher when cash is available. She further clarified that the procurement of goods and services shall proceed pursuant to RA No. 9184 and she is no longer required to sign the Purchase Request and Certificate of Cash Availability.
Auditor’s Rejoinder:
We believe that the reply of the City Treasurer was not responsive to the audit observation.  She should be cognizant of her responsibility that the moment she certified that cash is available, each obligation incurred is being assured of the corresponding actual cash.

We recommended that Management:

Direct the designated AOs to strictly comply with the rules and regulations particularly on the liquidation of CAs within the prescribed period to ensure that all disbursements are recorded in the books in the appropriate time/period;

clearance;
d.   Require the City Accountant to-

Whenever applicable condition exists, request for write-off of dormant unliquidated CAs which remained non-moving for 10 years or more and where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained in accordance with the guidelines and procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Management comment/action:
The City Accountant emphasize that her office is cognizant on the pertinent provision of COA Circular No. 97-002 and stressed that the decrease in the outstanding cash advances compared to the previous year was due to management’s efforts to monitor and control the grant of CAs and advised the end-users to resort to the normal mode of procurement prescribed under RA No. 9184. Further, the OCAT acknowledged the audit recommendations and assured that strict compliance with the prescribed rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances will be observed.

We recommended that Management facilitate the negotiation with the Service Provider of the City’s existing RPTAS, for the exclusion of the computation/assessment of the FCRT from the system; and let the BFP take charge of the assessment and collection of the prescribed fire code taxes, fees and charges, and fines.

However, she assured that the audit recommendation will be given utmost priority and importance.

We reiterated our recommendation that:
The City Budget Officer and the City Development Council of Manila re-appropriate for eligible development projects the savings/unexpended balance from the implementation of projects under the 20% DF pursuant to Section II. 5 of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 99-66 dated April 23, 1999, to attain maximum utilization of the fund;

Properly plan the timely procurement and implementation of all identified priority development projects under the 20% DF to guarantee the prompt accomplishment of the same.

The City Budget Officer explained that in compliance to the audit recommendation, she had requested from the CTO a certification as to cash availability for the unexpended balances of the 20% DF. As soon as the said certification is received by her Office, she will immediately forward her recommendation to the Local Development Council of Manila for the required programming of the said unexpended balances to priority development programs/projects.
OIC-City Engineer acknowledged the audit recommendation and explained that the delay in the execution of procurement activities was due to the late issuance of City Council Resolution, thus, it was only in December 2018 that Letters of Awards, Contracts and NTP were issued. He also informed that of the ten un-procured projects, six were already bidded while the remaining four are subject to realignment since the targeted projects were already implemented by the DPWH. Further, he assured that necessary actions will be made to execute the procurement and implementation of the projects within the target period.

25.8	We recommended that Management:

30.5	We recommended that Management:

Direct the City Accountant to analyze the balance of Due to BIR to determine the nature and composition of the account. If the balance pertains to unremitted taxes, then cause the immediate remittance to the BIR, otherwise, make the necessary adjustments.

Submit the JEVs covering the bank transactions for the SEF and GF to facilitate the audit.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that:
The CTO facilitate the updating of the City’s RPTAR/TIC to come-up with a comprehensive CLT which will be submitted to the OCAT as basis in the setting-up of the RPT and SET Receivables as required under Section 20 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I;
The DA expedite the completion of the cleansing and editing activity on all RPUs within the City’s jurisdiction to produce an official list of RPT delinquent accounts that will serve as basis in the enforcement of appropriate legal action to collect the same as provided under RA 7160. Use the subject list in adjusting the OCAT records to come-up with the precise balances of the RPT and SET Receivables; and
Management intensify collections of RPT that are due and demandable to augment the income necessary in the efficient discharge of its functions.

We recommended that Management:
Refrain from collecting FCRT and FSIF since the same is a mandated function of the BFP as clearly provided under RA      No. 9514. Likewise, coordinate and extend necessary support to the BFP for the smooth transition in the collection of the subject fire code taxes and fees through the provisions of enough space within the City’s premises for its collecting officers and staff; and
Direct the CTO to immediately remit the 80 percent share of the BFP from the collection of FSIF and FCRT to the BTr to ensure the allocation and utilization of the fund by the BFP.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned officials to facilitate the immediate utilization of the 20 percent share in the collection of the Fire Code taxes and fees for its intended purpose in order to strengthen the capabilities of the City’s local fire station in responding to disaster/calamities. Likewise, ensure that the 20 percent share of the City be set aside and retained for its immediate use.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive facilitate the creation and organization of the City’s independent IAS to aid Management in the efficient performance of its mandated functions and the effective management of the City’s operations as required under RA No. 4177.

We recommended that the City’s Personnel Selection Board ensure that the appointee is qualified for the position based on the requirements established under the City’s QS Manual, before issuance of appointment to avoid invalidation of the same. If warranted, a review of the City’s QS Manual be undertaken and recommend possible updates and/or revisions in accordance with Section 4, Rule 4 of the Omnibus Rules.

We recommended that Management:
Allocate the needed funds for the training expenses of all employees in each Department/Office of the City; and
Require the Human Resource and Development Office to conduct periodic training courses and seminars, and other related activities and programs for the continued development and enhancement of skills and experiences of all its employees.

We recommended that the OCAT strictly observe compliance with the submission of the monthly TBs and the Pre and Post-closing TBs at year-end, and FSs and other financial reports and schedules within the prescribed period as required under Section 70 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and Item 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010, to facilitate the review and validation of the fairness of presentation of the account balances in the FSs.

We recommended that the OCAT cause the immediate submission of all paid DVs which were long overdue to the CAO and, henceforth, strictly comply with the prescribed period of submission provided under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 to ensure the conduct of timely audit and review of the transactions and to communicate the results thereof to concerned officials.

We recommended that the CGSO and the DEPW regularly submit to the CAO copies of perfected contracts/POs/WOs and Notices of Delivery within the prescribed period as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996, respectively, for the conduct of timely audit of the said transactions. Likewise, ensure that all POs/WOs issued are properly accepted by the suppliers.

We recommended that the CTO require all COs to regularly prepare the RAAF and submit the same to the CTO-AFS to serve as basis in the preparation of the Consolidated RAAF and for the eventual submission of the same together with the individual reports of all COs to the CAO for the required verification and audit as mandated under Section 98 of the GAAM, Volume I and Sections 50 and 51 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that:
Management, through the Department of Social Welfare (DSW), CGSO and the OCAT, immediately submit to the CAO the pertinent data/supporting documents and DVs for the SFP to fully evaluate the success of the program and the propriety of the disbursement/s;
The DSW submit explanation on the variance between the number of target children beneficiaries, which is the basis in the computation of the funds released from the DSWD-NCR, as against the actual number of children beneficiaries served; and
Management strictly comply with the General Memorandum of Agreement (GMA)/Specific Implementation Agreements (SIA) particularly the stipulation on the immediate implementation of the program without unreasonable delay upon receipt of funds.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned City officials to determine/identify the reason/s for the disqualification of the interested applicants in the livelihood program and to extend necessary support and assistance or recommend appropriate measures to resolve or remedy the same without sacrificing the interest of the City and to promote success of the ETLP;
Require the concerned OCAT personnel to ensure the proper recording of all amortizations collected from the beneficiaries of the ETLP in the books of accounts and to regularly reconcile the SL with the CTO records so that any discrepancy/ies noted are immediately rectified; and
Make appropriate representation with the supplier for the immediate transfer of the CRs and ORs of the E-Trikes in favor of the City.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned City officials to coordinate with ICNS for the immediate modification and repair of the CTISM specifically on the eBarangay Computer and eHospital Management Systems to put them in good operating condition for its optimum utilization;
Make proper representation with the contractor for the conduct of extensive training for the hospital personnel to educate them in the proper way of operating the System; and
Require the EDP Office to facilitate the renewal or updating of the firewall’s license for its reconfiguration, as well as, the web server for the new IP address for the immediate realization of the on-line payment of real property and business taxes.

We recommended that all concerned City officials:
Closely monitor and ensure the efficient implementation of all priority development projects and programs under the 20% DF that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development of the City of Manila;
Coordinate closely with the DPWH and other NGAs to make sure that the projects to be incorporated in the City’s development plan are not among those projects listed to be implemented by the said agencies; and
Observe proper timing in the implementation of the infrastructure projects, for the implementing office to have ample time in accomplishing the projects and guarantee prompt completion for the benefits of the public.

We recommended that the City Budget Officer and the City Development Council of Manila ensure the re-appropriation for eligible development projects the prior years’ savings from the implementation of projects under the 20% DF, for its optimum utilization and for the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcome of the City.

We recommended that Management allocate adequate funds of at least 5% of their annual budget as mandated by existing laws, rules and regulations which should be integrated or incorporated in their regular PPAs for proper implementation of the GAD Plan in order to achieve its objectives towards gender equality.

We recommended that Management reconstitute the composition of the existing GFPS Execom and TWG in consonance with the provisions of Section 4.1A.2 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 in order to implement the GAD PPAs efficiently and effectively.

We recommended that the City Council, through the Committee on Women and Family, and with the assistance of GAD Focal Point System, prioritize the creation and passing of enabling ordinance or GAD Code pursuant to the afore-cited provisions of law, rules and regulations to support the City’s efforts in promoting, protecting and fulfilling women’s empowerment and gender equality.

We recommended that the GFPS, through the assistance of the CPDO, expedite the establishment of a GAD database to provide accurate information and bases for an effective gender planning, programming and policy formulation pursuant to Section 4.1.B.1 of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

We recommended that the MDRRMO facilitate the early preparation/formulation of the LDRRMFIP and other related reports in order that the planned DRRM PPAs are immediately implemented and guarantee the attainment of the City’s objective of mitigating the impacts of disaster/calamities towards building a disaster-resilient communities. Likewise, execute/undertake proper planning to ensure that all PPAs to be implemented are included in the approved LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the OCAT expedite the review and verification whether actual cash transfer from the GF to TF had transpired as regards the CYs 2010 and 2011 unexpended balance of DRRM Funds, so that appropriate correction or action be immediately undertaken to adjust/correct the same.

We recommended that the OCAT regularly prepare and provide copies of RSUDRRMF to the MDRRMO so that the said implementing Office will be well informed of the accurate balance/amount of DRRM Funds available for appropriation and for the concerned offices to closely coordinate with one another for an efficient and effective implementation of the PPAs pertaining to risk reduction preparedness and mitigation.

We recommended that the MDRRMO incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the 30% QRF to present the actual amount of DRRM Funds appropriated and available for utilization. Likewise, properly indicate in the LDRRMFIP the funding source of the PPAs proposed to be implemented for proper charging and recording in the books.
We recommended that Management ensure strict compliance on the submission of monthly RSU of the LDRRMF to the CAO as required under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that Management appropriate in the LGUs Annual Budget 1% of its IRA as required by RA No. 9344 for the strengthening and implementation of PPAs of the LCPC.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure the regular, accurate and timely remittance in full of all the deductions withheld from employees for GSIS contributions and amortizations including the City share to comply with the requirements of the law and to avoid incurrence of interests and penalties; and
Effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct balances of the Due to GSIS and Cash in Bank - Local Currency, Current Account.

We recommended that Management exhaust all efforts and available means allowed by law, rules and regulations to impose the required compliance by the persons identified to be liable with the audit suspension and for the collection or settlement of the reported/recorded disallowances and charges pursuant to the provisions of the RRSA.
